title: Bishop Springs the Battery
setup: A bishop and rook are stacked on the **e-file** in front of the enemy king — a textbook battery aimed straight at e8.
hint: The bishop blocks more than it defends — move it with check, and let the rook do the heavy lifting.
explanation:
## The Idea

When two of your long-range pieces line up behind one another, pointing at the enemy king, you have a **battery**. The piece in front is the mover; the piece behind is the discoverer. Here the bishop on e6 is the mover and the rook on e4 is the discoverer.

## Why {{Bd7+}} Is Devastating

The bishop slides to d7 and gives check by itself along the d7-e8 diagonal. **At the same instant**, by leaving e6, it uncovers the rook on e4 — which now also checks the king down the e-file. Two pieces are checking the black king at once.

- This is **double check**: blocking is impossible.
- Capturing the bishop is impossible — there is no black piece nearby that reaches d7.
- Capturing the rook is impossible — the black king cannot reach e4.
- The king **must** move.

## What Goes Wrong Without Check

A quiet bishop move (say, Bb3) would still uncover the rook, but the rook check is single, and Black can simply step the king to d7 or f7 — even capture the bishop in some lines. The point of {{Bd7+}} is that the bishop **arrives with tempo**: by checking on its own, it forces Black to handle two threats with one move, which the rules of chess forbid.
